costs: Network consolidation and school-closure administrative costs, driven by demographic necessity rather than policy design, magnitude unquantified at settlement level though KSH/Oktatási Hivatal aggregate trends exist.
Teacher workforce redeployment or attrition costs associated with consolidation, potentially displacing experienced teachers from closing small schools, magnitude unquantified.

Foregone equity-investment opportunity cost: since this baseline involves no active compensatory spending, it carries zero direct fiscal cost but a substantial implicit cost in unaddressed segregation relative to any of S1-S4.
content.equity_impact: Passive contraction alone does not address, and may worsen, the concentration of remaining selective places among already-advantaged urban and church-maintained institutions, since consolidation pressure falls hardest on smaller, often rural or general-school providers while urban elite gimnáziums (44.8% of secondary enrollment nationally, concentrated in Budapest) retain sufficient demand to persist; without any active equity lever, the SES-skew of who accesses the shrinking number of selective places is likely to remain unchanged or worsen, and this scenario explicitly does nothing about the documented near-zero admission share of multiply-disadvantaged pupils.
expected_benefits: Provides a clean counterfactual baseline against which the incremental effects of S1-S4 can be measured, isolating policy-driven change from demographically-driven change.

Reduces the absolute number of selective-track places over time simply through cohort shrinkage, without requiring any legislative or administrative action, avoiding all the implementation and reversal risks documented for S1-S4.

May force some degree of network consolidation that incidentally reduces the number of dispersed, small selective-track offerings in thin rural markets, though this is a side effect of viability thresholds rather than a designed equity mechanism.
content.goal: Serve as the no-new-policy counterfactual baseline against which S1-S4 are measured, in which shrinking birth cohorts force school-network consolidation and a natural decline in the selective track's absolute size and share regardless of any active equity or structural intervention.

implementation_steps.action: Continue publishing annual demographic and enrollment statistics tracking cohort decline and its mechanical effect on track sizes, without introducing new admission, funding, or structural levers.
implementation_steps.actor: KSH / Ministry of Education (monitoring only, no active policy design)

implementation_steps.action: Respond reactively to falling enrollment through ad hoc, maintainer-level consolidation decisions as individual schools fall below viability thresholds, absent any coordinated national plan.
Track and publish the counterfactual comparison data (selective-track share, SES composition, achievement gaps) needed to evaluate S1-S4 against this baseline.

mechanisms: Annual births fell from ~125,000 (early 1990s) to 77,500 (2024) and ~72,000 (2025 estimate), the lowest recorded level since 1949, mechanically shrinking the pool of children available to enter any secondary track, selective or not.

A measurable demographic cliff one cohort ahead (approximately 17,000 fewer one-year-olds than four-year-olds, equivalent to 600-800 kindergarten/class-groups) means at least one hundred small schools' worth of children will structurally vanish from the system within a few years, independent of any policy choice.

Physical school-network capacity (880 secondary schools, underused dormitory capacity) is not the binding constraint on selective-track viability; the binding constraint is the shrinking and unevenly distributed child population itself, meaning passive demographic contraction alone will not resolve the geographic concentration of selection in a minority of (mostly urban, church-maintained) settlements.
risks: Politically the path of least resistance, since it requires no legislative action and therefore faces no direct Fidesz culture-war framing risk, but this apparent political safety is itself a risk: it allows policymakers to avoid the equity problem indefinitely while claiming demographic forces are 'naturally' solving selectivity, when the evidence shows government is simultaneously using score thresholds (S2) as an active supply lever layered atop passive contraction.

Absence of active intervention risks EU-level criticism given the European Commission's explicit 2025 finding that Hungary's system amplifies social and economic inequalities, potentially triggering funds-conditionality pressure regardless of demographic trends.

Reactive, uncoordinated maintainer-level consolidation (as opposed to planned network restructuring) risks arbitrary and inequitable closure patterns, disproportionately affecting rural general schools while urban elite gimnáziums are insulated by sustained demand.
content.title: Demographically-driven passive contraction (status-quo baseline)
